failure_surface: All code execution attempts resulted in SyntaxError: invalid syntax at line 1, with the error pointing to a '<tool_call>' character instead of valid Python code.
root_cause: The code being sent to the execution environment contained embedded tool-call markers or improperly formatted characters that prevented Python from parsing the script. The repeated SyntaxError at line 1 across multiple algorithmic variations (for loops, while loops, explicit lists) indicates the issue is not with the logic but with code submission/formatting.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before executing any generated Python code, strip any non-code characters (such as tool invocation markers or hidden unicode characters) from the code string to ensure only valid Python syntax is passed to the interpreter.
evidence: Five consecutive execution attempts all failed with identical SyntaxError: invalid syntax at Cell In[1], line 1
The error traceback specifically points to '<tool_call>' character at the beginning of line 1
Three different looping constructs were tried (for i in range, while loop, explicit list) - all failed identically
The Python logic itself (COUNTIFS formula concatenation) would be syntactically valid if isolated from the execution wrapper

memory_items.title: Code Sanitization Before Execution
memory_items.lesson: Always validate that generated code contains only valid Python syntax before passing to the execution engine.
memory_items.generalization: Strip tool-markup characters, invisible unicode, or wrapper artifacts from code strings prior to execution to prevent parser-level failures.

memory_items.title: Iterative Retry Without Validation
memory_items.lesson: Changing algorithmic approach without fixing root cause yields repeated failures of the same type.
memory_items.generalization: When encountering the same error across multiple attempts, diagnose the common denominator (code format, not logic) rather than varying the implementation strategy.

memory_items.title: Openpyxl Formula Assignment Patterns
memory_items.lesson: Assigning formulas via cell.value works correctly when syntax issues are resolved.
memory_items.generalization: Use openpyxl cell.value for formula injection with absolute/relative reference notation ($A$2:$A5) preserved during dynamic row iteration.

edits.content:
### 7. Code Execution Hygiene
- **Sanitize Before Running:** Strip any non-code characters (tool markup, invisible unicode, or wrapper artifacts) from generated scripts before execution.
- **Validate Syntax First:** Ensure the code string contains only valid Python syntax; test in isolation if errors persist across multiple logic variations.
- **Common Failure Pattern:** If the same SyntaxError occurs on every attempt regardless of algorithm changes, investigate the code transmission layer rather than the implementation logic.
